algorithms.name: Activity Selection
algorithms.analogy: A meeting room with back-to-back meetings. You want to fit as many non-overlapping meetings as possible. The greedy insight: always pick the meeting that ENDS EARLIEST. By choosing early-ending meetings, you leave maximum room for future meetings. Sort by end time, then greedily pick each meeting that starts after the previous one ends.
algorithms.story: Activity Selection is the canonical greedy proof. The exchange argument proves it: if there's an optimal solution that doesn't include the earliest-ending activity, you can swap it in without making things worse (since it ends even earlier, it creates at least as much room). This proof technique applies to all greedy algorithms.
algorithms.whenToUse: Non-overlapping intervals, maximum number of meetings/events, meeting rooms required, jump game, minimum number of arrows to burst balloons.
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Sort intervals by end time
2. Select first interval (earliest end)
3. For each subsequent interval:
   If interval.start >= last selected interval.end:
     Select it (update last end time)
     Increment count
4. Return count
algorithms.interviewTips: Know three interval variants cold: max non-overlapping (sort by end, greedy pick), min meeting rooms (two sorted arrays + two pointers), merge intervals (sort by start, extend end). The key distinction: sort by END for counting/selection, sort by START for merging.
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Meeting Rooms I/II","desc":"Meeting Rooms I/II (LeetCode 252/253)"},{"title":"Non-overlapping Intervals","desc":"Non-overlapping Intervals (LeetCode 435)"},{"title":"Merge Intervals","desc":"Merge Intervals (LeetCode 56)"},{"title":"Minimum arrows to burst balloons","desc":"Minimum arrows to burst balloons (LeetCode 452)"}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Sorting by start instead of end for activity selection","Using > instead of >= for the start/end comparison","Meeting rooms: not accounting for simultaneous end/start (a[0] < ends vs a[0] <= ends)"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Min meeting rooms","desc":"Min meeting rooms"},{"name":"Merge intervals","desc":"Merge intervals"},{"name":"Insert interval","desc":"Insert interval"},{"name":"Minimum arrows to burst balloons","desc":"Minimum arrows to burst balloons"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Sort by END TIME for maximum activities. Sort by START TIME for merging intervals. This distinction is the single most important detail — wrong sort criterion gives wrong answers. Min meeting rooms = max simultaneous overlapping intervals = two sorted arrays + two pointers.
algorithms.patternSignal: maximum non-overlapping intervals, activity selection, meeting rooms, sort by end time, interval scheduling, minimum rooms, non-overlapping
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Non-overlapping Intervals","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Sort by end time. Greedily keep intervals — remove one when overlap detected (always remove the later-ending one)"},{"name":"Meeting Rooms II","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Sort starts and ends separately. Two pointers: if start < end → need new room; else free one"},{"name":"Minimum Number of Arrows to Burst Balloons","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Sort by end — each arrow hits as many overlapping balloons as possible"},{"name":"Merge Intervals","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Sort by start. Extend end if overlap, else add current and move to next"},{"name":"Maximum CPU Load","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Sort by start, use min-heap of end times to track active intervals"}]

algorithms.name: Jump Game
algorithms.analogy: Walking on stepping stones where each stone tells you the maximum distance you can jump. Track the furthest point you CAN reach as you walk. If at any stone you're beyond your reachable range (meaning you've fallen into a gap), return false. If you make it to the last stone within your reachable range, return true.
algorithms.story: Jump Game I is the greediest of greedy — maintain a single variable `maxReach`. At each position i, if i > maxReach you're stuck (false). Otherwise update maxReach = max(maxReach, i + nums[i]). Jump Game II (minimum jumps) uses a 'current level end' and 'next level end' pattern — classic BFS-on-implicit-graph thinking applied as greedy.
algorithms.whenToUse: Can-reach-end problems, minimum steps/jumps, ladder game, any problem where local maximum determines global reachability.
algorithms.howItWorks:
Jump Game I (can reach end?):
1. maxReach = 0
2. For i = 0 to n-1:
   If i > maxReach → return false
   maxReach = max(maxReach, i + nums[i])
3. Return true

Jump Game II (min jumps):
1. jumps=0, currEnd=0, farthest=0
2. For i = 0 to n-2:
   farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i])
   If i == currEnd: jumps++; currEnd = farthest
3. Return jumps
algorithms.interviewTips: Jump Game II's greedy is essentially BFS levels: currEnd is the boundary of the current BFS level. When you reach it, you must jump (increment count) and extend the boundary to farthest. The loop goes to n-2 (not n-1) because you don't need to jump FROM the last position.
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Jump Game I/II/VI","desc":"Jump Game I/II/VI (LeetCode 55/45/1696)"},{"title":"Reach the last index","desc":"Reach the last index"},{"title":"Video stitching","desc":"Video stitching"},{"title":"Minimum taps to water garden","desc":"Minimum taps to water garden"}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Jump Game I: checking after updating maxReach (should check i > maxReach FIRST)","Jump Game II: looping to n-1 instead of n-2 (double-counts last jump)","Not updating farthest before checking i==currEnd"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Jump Game II","desc":"Jump Game II (min jumps)"},{"name":"Jump Game III","desc":"Jump Game III (can reach 0)"},{"name":"Jump Game VI","desc":"Jump Game VI (max score)"},{"name":"Video stitching","desc":"Video stitching"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Jump Game I: track maxReach — if i > maxReach, stuck (return false immediately). Jump Game II: currEnd marks BFS level boundary — increment jumps when i reaches currEnd. Loop to n-2 (not n-1) — don't need to jump FROM the last position.
algorithms.patternSignal: can reach end, minimum jumps, farthest reach, greedy reach, reachability problem, stepping stones, ladder game
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Jump Game","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Track maxReach. If i > maxReach → stuck. Update maxReach = max(maxReach, i+nums[i])"},{"name":"Jump Game II","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"BFS levels: farthest=max in current range. When i==currEnd: jump++, currEnd=farthest"},{"name":"Jump Game III","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"BFS/DFS: from each position jump to i+nums[i] or i-nums[i]. Find if any position has nums[i]==0"},{"name":"Video Stitching","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Jump game variant: each clip [start,end] is a jump from start. Greedy: extend coverage maximally"}]

algorithms.name: Gas Station
algorithms.analogy: Driving around a circular route of gas stations. At each station, you get some gas but the trip to the next station costs some gas. Can you complete the loop? If total gas >= total cost, a solution exists. The starting point is the station after the last point where cumulative tank went negative — that's where a fresh start can succeed.
algorithms.story: Gas Station has two key insights: (1) if sum(gas) >= sum(cost), exactly one valid starting point exists. (2) To find it, track the running tank. Whenever it goes negative, reset: the starting point must be i+1 (anywhere from old start to i is invalid). The total deficit accumulates in a separate variable to check overall feasibility.
algorithms.whenToUse: Circular array problems where you need to find a starting point, any problem about feasibility of a circular traversal.
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. totalGas = 0, tank = 0, start = 0
2. For i = 0 to n-1:
   diff = gas[i] - cost[i]
   tank += diff
   totalGas += diff
   If tank < 0:
     start = i + 1  (reset start)
     tank = 0       (reset current tank)
3. Return totalGas >= 0 ? start : -1
algorithms.interviewTips: The correctness proof: if [0..i] has negative total, no station in that range can be the start. The candidate start keeps moving forward. At the end, if total >= 0, the last candidate start is the answer. This O(n) solution is often asked to follow up on the obvious O(n²) brute force.
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Gas Station","desc":"Gas Station (LeetCode 134)"},{"title":"Circular buffer feasibility","desc":"Circular buffer feasibility"},{"title":"Fleet scheduling","desc":"Fleet scheduling"}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Forgetting to check totalGas >= 0 at the end (the candidate start may be valid locally but global sum is negative)","Resetting totalGas along with tank (they track different things)"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Minimum cost to fill gas","desc":"Minimum cost to fill gas"},{"name":"Circular trip feasibility","desc":"Circular trip feasibility"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: If totalGas >= totalCost, a solution EXISTS. Valid start = index AFTER last point where running tank went negative. Keep totalGas separate from tank — totalGas checks global feasibility, tank finds the local starting candidate.
algorithms.patternSignal: circular route, gas station, starting point feasibility, circular array traversal, find valid starting position, circular loop
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Gas Station","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Track totalGas and tank. Reset start=i+1 and tank=0 whenever tank<0. Return start if totalGas>=0"},{"name":"Circular Candy Distribution","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Same circular feasibility check — find the starting position where cumulative never goes negative"}]

algorithms.name: Fractional Knapsack
algorithms.analogy: Filling a backpack with gold nuggets of different sizes and values. You can take fractions of each nugget (unlike 0/1 knapsack). The greedy strategy: take from the most valuable nugget per gram first. Sort by value/weight ratio descending. Fill greedily. If a full item doesn't fit, take the fraction that does.
algorithms.story: Fractional knapsack is the greedy version of the knapsack problem. It's solvable greedily (unlike 0/1 knapsack which needs DP) because you can take partial items. Proof: if you don't take the highest ratio item first, you can always swap it in and improve the solution. Sort by value/weight descending, greedily take until knapsack is full.
algorithms.whenToUse: Fractional items optimization, task scheduling by priority/time ratio, any 'best ratio first' greedy problems.
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Calculate value/weight ratio for each item
2. Sort items by ratio descending
3. While capacity > 0 and items remain:
   If item.weight <= remaining capacity:
     Take full item: value += item.value, capacity -= item.weight
   Else:
     Take fraction: value += item.value × (capacity/item.weight), capacity = 0
4. Return total value
algorithms.interviewTips: Interviewers often ask about 0/1 vs fractional knapsack — key point: greedy works for fractional (exchange argument), but 0/1 needs DP because you can't take fractions. Task Scheduler is a related greedy problem often appearing together.
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Fractional knapsack","desc":"Fractional knapsack"},{"title":"Task scheduler","desc":"Task scheduler (LeetCode 621)"},{"title":"Job scheduling with deadlines","desc":"Job scheduling with deadlines"},{"title":"Maximum profit allocation","desc":"Maximum profit allocation"}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Applying greedy to 0/1 knapsack — doesn't work","Integer division in ratio calculation (use double cast)","Taking full capacity when item.weight > remaining"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"0/1 Knapsack","desc":"0/1 Knapsack (DP)"},{"name":"Task Scheduler","desc":"Task Scheduler"},{"name":"Job scheduling with deadlines","desc":"Job scheduling with deadlines"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Fractional knapsack is GREEDY (take highest value/weight ratio first). 0/1 knapsack is DP (can't take fractions). This distinction is the most frequently tested concept on this problem. Exchange argument proves greedy correctness for fractional variant.
algorithms.patternSignal: fractional knapsack, best ratio first, value per weight, take portions, maximize value with capacity, greedy optimization, ratio sorting
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Fractional Knapsack","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Sort by value/weight ratio desc. Take full items until capacity, then take fraction of next item"},{"name":"Task Scheduler","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Formula: max(tasks.length, (maxFreq-1)*(n+1)+countOfMaxFreq). Always idle after most frequent task"},{"name":"Minimize Maximum of Array","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Binary search on answer + greedy check — O(n log max)"},{"name":"Maximum Units on a Truck","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Sort by units/box desc, greedily take boxes until truck is full"}]
